### 1. Current experimental status – detectors still running or recently finished  

- **Super‑Kamiokande (SK)** is the most sensitive proton‑decay experiment; data taking continues through 2023.  
  *Source:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Proton_decay  

- **Hyper‑Kamiokande (HK)** is under construction and will begin operation in the late 2020s.  
  *Source:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Proton_decay  

- **Deep Underground Neutrino Experiment (DUNE)** far detector will be completed at SURF and is scheduled to start physics runs in 2026–27.  
  *Source:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Deep_Underground_Neutrino_Experiment  

- **Jiangmen Underground Neutrino Observatory (JUNO)** is primarily a reactor‑neutrino experiment but its large volume and depth make it a potential future proton‑decay facility; construction is underway with first data expected around 2025.  
  *Source:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Deep_Underground_Neutrino_Experiment  

---

### 2. Strongest published exclusion limit for the dominant mode \(p \rightarrow e^{+}\pi^{0}\)  

- **Super‑Kamiokande**: lifetime limit \(\tau/B(p\to e^{+}\pi^{0}) > 1.6\times10^{34}\,\text{yr}\) (90 % C.L.) from data collected up to 2017.  
  *Source:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Proton_decay  

- **Super‑Kamiokande Phase IV**: lifetime limit \(\tau/B(p\to e^{+}\pi^{0}) > 2.4\times10^{34}\,\text{yr}\) (90 % C.L.) from data taken through 2023.  
  *Source:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Proton_decay  

---

### 3. Strongest published exclusion limit for the dominant mode \(p \rightarrow \bar{\nu}K^{+}\)  

- **Super‑Kamiokande**: lifetime limit \(\tau/B(p\to \bar{\nu}K^{+}) > 5.9\times10^{33}\,\text{yr}\) (90 % C.L.) from data collected up to 2018.  
  *Source:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Proton_decay  

---

### 4. Projected sensitivity of Hyper‑Kamiokande  

- **\(p \rightarrow e^{+}\pi^{0}\)**: projected lifetime limit \(\tau/B(p\to e^{+}\pi^{0}) \sim 2\times10^{35}\,\text{yr}\) after ~20 yr exposure.  
  *Source:* https://arxiv.org/abs/2010.16098  

- **\(p \rightarrow \bar{\nu}K^{+}\)**: projected lifetime limit \(\tau/B(p\to \bar{\nu}K^{+}) \sim 1\times10^{35}\,\text{yr}\) after ~20 yr exposure.  
  *Source:* https://arxiv.org/abs/2010.16098

  *Source:* https://inspirehep.net/literature/2634575  

---

### 6. JUNO – no published proton‑decay limit yet; potential future facility  

- **JUNO** has not yet reported an experimental limit for proton decay, but its large volume and depth make it a candidate for future searches.  
  *Source:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Deep_Underground_Neutrino_Experiment  

---

### 7. Neutron–antineutron oscillation limits  

- **ILL free‑neutron experiment**: lower bound \(\tau_{n\rightarrow\bar{n}} > 0.86\times10^{8}\,\text{s}\) (≈ 2.7 yr, 90 % C.L.).

## 1. Introduction  
The study under review presents a Random Forest (RF) model tailored for sales forecasting across multiple retail locations, emphasizing the incorporation of local cultural factors—holidays, salary days, protests, and weather conditions—as exogenous variables to enhance predictive performance.

The authors implemented a Random Forest model and employed **randomized search cross‑validation** for hyperparameter tuning, systematically exploring a wide range of parameter combinations to identify the configuration that maximizes predictive accuracy.  
- **Source:** https://arxiv.org/abs/2410.13773  

### 2.2 Dataset Composition  
The dataset incorporates exogenous variables reflecting local cultural factors—holidays, salary days, protests, and weather conditions—to capture temporal patterns influencing retail sales.  
- **Source:** https://arxiv.org/abs/2410.13773

### 3.1 Model Performance

- **Source:** https://arxiv.org/abs/2410.13773  

### 3.2 Comparison with Other Models

- Gradient Boosting (R‑squared: **0.942**)

- Details on dataset provenance, feature encoding, and author affiliations are absent, hindering reproducibility.  
- No peer‑reviewed corroboration was identified within the current search scope.  
- **Source:** Peer Review Summary (see findings.md)  

## 5. Conclusion  
The study demonstrates that a Random Forest model, when optimized via randomized search cross‑validation and enriched with local cultural factors, can achieve high R‑squared values relative to several advanced machine learning models. However, the lack of peer review, limited dataset transparency, and absence of external validation necessitate further research before broad application.

- **Random Forest** - A tree-based ensemble learning method that uses a random subset of features to build decision trees, which can be used for sales forecasting.
- **Gradient Boosting Machines (GBM)** - These algorithms use an iterative process to build models in a gradient boosting framework, often used for regression and classification tasks.
- **Support Vector Machines (SVM)** - A powerful classification algorithm that uses a hyperplane to separate data points in a high-dimensional space, useful for sales prediction when dealing with complex datasets.
- **K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN)** - A simple yet effective algorithm for classification and regression problems. It is used in sales forecasting by finding the nearest neighbors of a given data point.
- **Decision Trees** - A tree-like model of decisions and their possible outcomes, often used in sales prediction to classify and predict based on decision rules.

### **Heuristic Algorithms for Sales Prediction**
- **N-BEATS (Neural Basis Expansion Analysis)** - A deep learning-based method that uses neural networks to forecast future values.
- **N-HiTS (Neural Hybrid Time Series)** - Combines traditional statistical methods with deep learning techniques to predict sales data.
- **Temporal Fusion Transformer (TFT)** - A deep learning model used for time series forecasting, which can be applied in sales prediction scenarios.
- **XGBoost** - An ensemble machine learning system that uses gradient boosting to make predictions.
- **LightGBM** - An optimized gradient boosting framework that is efficient and effective for data with missing values.

# Top 5 Heuristic Methods Frequently Applied to Improve Sales Prediction Models in Retail Franchise Settings

## Introduction
Retail franchise chains often face limited historical data, high variability across outlets, and the need for rapid decision‑making. To address these challenges, many organizations employ heuristic methods—simple, experience‑based rules or optimization techniques that can be implemented quickly and with minimal computational resources. The following report summarizes five of the most commonly used heuristics in this context, drawing on recent literature.

## 1. Rule‑of‑Thumb Heuristics
**Description:**  
Simple, experience‑based rules such as “sales rise by X % during the holiday season.” These are intuitive shortcuts that do not rely on statistical modeling.

**Typical Use Case:**  
A franchise chain may apply a rule that increases forecasted sales by 15 % for all outlets during December to account for holiday shopping spikes. This is often used when historical data is sparse or as an initial baseline before more complex models are built.

**Source:**  
- **[MDPI – Heuristic Methods in Sales Forecasting for Retail Franchise](https://www.mdpi.com/2571-9394/6/3/28)**

## 2. Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO)
**Description:**  
An evolutionary optimization technique that simulates swarm behavior to search high‑dimensional parameter spaces.

**Typical Use Case:**  
PSO is used to tune hyper‑parameters of forecasting models such as ARIMA or neural networks, or to select the best subset of predictors for a franchise’s demand model. By iteratively adjusting candidate solutions, PSO converges on parameter sets that minimize forecast error.

**Source:**  
- **[MDPI – Heuristic Methods in Sales Forecasting for Retail Franchise](https://www.mdpi.com/2571-9394/6/3/28)**

## 3. Genetic Algorithms (GA)
**Description:**  
Evolutionary method that evolves a population of candidate solutions through selection, crossover, and mutation.

**Typical Use Case:**  
GAs are employed to optimize product‑mix decisions, pricing strategies, or promotion schedules for individual franchise outlets. They can also fine‑tune model parameters in complex forecasting pipelines where traditional gradient‑based methods struggle.

**Source:**  
- **[MDPI – Heuristic Methods in Sales Forecasting for Retail Franchise](https://www.mdpi.com/2571-9394/6/3/28)**

## 4. Moving‑Average (MA) Smoothing
**Description:**  
A simple heuristic that averages sales over a fixed window of past periods to smooth short‑term fluctuations.

**Typical Use Case:**  
Retail franchises often use a 3‑month moving average to generate baseline forecasts for inventory planning, especially when data is noisy or seasonality is weak. The MA can be updated daily or weekly as new sales arrive.

**Source:**  
- **[Forecast.io – 10 Sales Forecasting Methods](https://forecastio.ai/blog/10-sales-forecasting-methods)**

## 5. Weather/Holiday Adjustment Heuristic
**Description:**  
A rule‑based adjustment that modifies forecasts based on weather conditions or holiday schedules.

**Typical Use Case:**  
While the full details are truncated in the source, such heuristics typically increase or decrease predicted sales when adverse weather is forecasted or during major holidays to capture expected consumer behavior changes.

**Source:**  
- **[MDPI – Heuristic Methods in Sales Forecasting for Retail Franchise](https://www.mdpi.com/2571-9394/6/3/28)**

# Final Report on the Construction Timeline of the Eiffel Tower

## Introduction
The Eiffel Tower, an iconic symbol of Paris and a marvel of 19th‑century engineering, was completed in the late 1880s. This report consolidates verified findings from authoritative sources regarding its completion year, exact date, construction duration, and assembly timeline.

## Findings
1. **Completion Year** – The tower was finished in **1889**.
   - Source: [Official Eiffel Tower website](https://www.toureiffel.paris/en/the-monument/history) – “On the 31st March 1889, the Tower had been finished …” (lines 11‑13).
   - Source: [Wikipedia – Eiffel Tower](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Eiffel_Tower) – “The tower was completed in **1889**, with the official completion date recorded as **March 31, 1889**.”

2. **Exact Completion Date** – Construction work finished on **March 31, 1889**.
   - Source: [Official Eiffel Tower website](https://www.toureiffel.paris/en/the-monument/history) – “The construction work began in January 1887 and was finished on **March 31, 1889**.” (lines 118‑120).

3. **Construction Duration** – The total build time was *2 years, 2 months and 5 days*, spanning from **26 January 1887** to **31 March 1889**.
   - Source: [Official Eiffel Tower website](https://www.toureiffel.paris/en/the-monument/history).

4. **Assembly of Supports Claim** – The official site states that the assembly of supports began on **1 July 1887** and was completed twenty‑two months later.
   - Source: [Official Eiffel Tower website](https://www.toureiffel.paris/en/the-monument/history).
   - Note: This specific claim is not corroborated by Wikipedia or other cited sources; historical records indicate that assembly started earlier in 1887 and concluded around September 1888. Further verification from primary archival documents is required.

## Analysis & Notes
- The convergence of the official Eiffel Tower website and Wikipedia confirms both the completion year (1889) and the exact date (March 31, 1889).

1. **Verify Completion Date** – Consult primary archival documents such as original construction contracts or French Ministry of Culture records to corroborate the exact day and any ceremonial details.
